properties.title: The New Popular Front
properties.abstract:
The ascendancy of the authoritarian right has generated a counterreaction on the part of the Left to restore the Popular Front that once confronted fascism. Although often led by the socialist left, the Popular Front was the vanguard of the movement to democratize capitalism rather than abolish it. Is recreating such a front the only or best way to confront the threat of fascism today?

Democracy is still on its trial, but so far it has not disgraced itself; it is true that its full force has no

properties.title: What’s Wrong With the German Left?
properties.abstract:
Far from intervening responsibly against the Israeli genocide, the German left has fallen in with the German state in suppressing dissent and backing Israel. This cannot be attributed to historical memory or political miscalculation. Its gross participation in ginning up moral panic around spurious accusations of antisemitism is rather an extraordinary abdication of its moral duty.

While the global movement in solidarity with Palestine has been repressed more or less everywhere, the German mobi

properties.title: Writing the Climate Crisis
properties.abstract:
This essay examines how cultural theory displaces capitalism in its accounts of the climate crisis. Contrasting Amitav Ghosh’s Gun Island with Matt Haig’s The Life Impossible, I argue that their differing approaches reveal what much cultural analysis leaves unexamined: the structural ties between ecological breakdown and global capital.

As the climate crisis continues to unfold on a global scale, the culture industry has devoted substantial attention to it.1 Some is in the realm of theory, in w

properties.title: The Courts and American Capitalism
properties.abstract:
A functional analysis of the Supreme Court as a node within a larger project of hegemonic preservation, this essay clarifies the nature of judicial power and recalibrates the terms of the court reform debate by bringing it into conversation with a longer tradition of left theory.

properties.title: The Left Has Always Fought for Abundance
properties.abstract:
The Left once promised plenty for all. Now liberals call for abundance while socialists hesitate and environmentalists shudder. The NGO-industrial complex these liberals target is indeed a fetter on production that must be broken, but so is the profitability demanded by capital. Socialism needs abundance, and abundance also needs socialism.

Socialism means plenty for all. We do not preach a gospel of want and scarcity, but of abundance. — Suffragette and socialist Sylvia Pankhurst1 Ezra Klein a

properties.abstract:
According to many on the Left, the set of feasible income distributions within a capitalist economy is tightly constrained by capitalists’ control over investment. Our empirical analysis of the postwar US economy raises significant doubts about this view. Class struggle and the power resources of the working class can affect the long-run distribution of incomes between classes.

properties.title: Lula’s Popular Front
properties.abstract:
This article analyzes Luiz Inácio Lula da Silva’s embrace of a broad front against far-right forces associated with former president Jair Bolsonaro. It explores Lula’s ideologically expansive 2022 presidential campaign and his challenges governing amid partisan fragmentation and argues that broad coalitions, while effective electorally, complicate progressive governance in polarized democracies.

properties.title: Technofeudalism Is Just Capitalism
properties.abstract:
Cédric Durand and Yanis Varoufakis have recently argued that the capitalist mode of production is being replaced by a more exploitative and less productive mode of production that they call techno-feudalism. But their case depends on implausible claims about the constitution of classes, the status of online platform users, and the sources of rent extraction. Capitalism is alive and well.

properties.title: Why the Democrats Are So Useless
properties.abstract:
The Democratic Party’s transformation into a network of policy clientele organizations has left it incapacitated as an opposition party. These groups, which lack strong mass-membership bases and sources of structural power, have struggled to respond to Donald Trump’s attack on the state. Turning the tide will require transcending the politics of programmatic liberalism.

The first few weeks of Donald Trump’s second presidential term began — as did so many events that defined his first four years

properties.title: The Fantasy of Revolution
properties.abstract:
Socialist revolutions have all but disap-peared during the past half century, even if they remain a remote possibility in authoritarian countries with corrupt, long-standing autocrats. By contrast, the achievements of broad social and democratic rights have rendered insurrectionary politics a strategic cul-de-sac in advanced capitalist countries. The era of revolutions is likely over.

The Left has always had a special interest in revolutions, at least since the time of Karl Marx. That’s because

properties.title: The Democrats Embrace Dealignment
properties.abstract:
In the last 20 years, Democrats have worked diligently to bring their electoral strategy into line with their Clinton-era neoliberal economic program. Class dealignment was therefore no accident — it was the foreseeable (and foreseen) consequence of changes made by Democrats in their economic and electoral strategy.

properties.title: The Latino Rebuke
properties.abstract:
Latino voters’ decisive contribution to Donald Trump’s return to power has puzzled progressive analysts. An analysis of Latinos’ material insecurity and labor market vulnerability shows that rejecting the Democrats and exploring entry into the MAGA coalition, while alarming, was predictable. Like blue-collar white voters in 2016, working-class Latinos did not embrace Trump because they are sexist xenophobes but rather because they lack alternatives for effectively defending their interests.

properties.abstract:
While Donald Trump’s domination of the Republican Party is unprecedented in many ways, it has not altered the fundamental dynamics in the party, which predate his takeover. Since 2008, the party has been characterized by increasing internal conflict and institutional weakening. Far from counteracting these trends, Trump’s rule over the party has intensified both.

properties.abstract: The claim that the human body is separable from social relations deserves an immanent Marxist critique. properties.name: Platform Capitalism
properties.definition: Wirtschaftssystem, in dem digitale Plattformen als intermediäre Märkte fungieren und Daten als zentrale Ressource extrahieren (Srnicek, 2016)

properties.name: Surveillance Capitalism
properties.definition: Form des Kapitalismus, die auf der systematischen Überwachung von Verhalten zur Gewinnmaximierung basiert (Zuboff, 2019)

properties.name: Gig Economy
properties.definition: Arbeitsmarktstruktur mit kurzfristigen, flexiblen Arbeitsverhältnissen über digitale Plattformen

properties.name: Data Extractivism
properties.definition: Extraktion von Daten als Rohstoff durch Plattformen, oft aus dem Globalen Süden

properties.name: Precarious Work
properties.definition: Unsichere, flexible Arbeit ohne soziale Absicherung, typisch für Gig Economy

properties.name: Labor Organizing in Tech
properties.definition: Gewerkschaftliche Organisierung von Tech-Arbeitern und Plattform-Arbeitern

properties.title: Kapitel: Platform Capitalism Theorie
properties.description: Theoretische Grundlegung: Srnicek, Zuboff, historische Entwicklung der Plattformökonomie

properties.title: Empirische Analyse: Gig Economy Arbeitsbedingungen
properties.description: Analyse von 542 Arbeitswelt-Artikeln: Uber, Lieferando, Amazon Flex, Prekarisierung

properties.title: Fallstudien: Labor Organizing in Tech
properties.description: Tech Workers Coalition, Amazon Labor Union, Alphabet Workers Union - Erfolge und Hindernisse

properties.title: KI-Einfluss auf Arbeitswelt
EU Regulation: DMA, DSA, AI Act
properties.description: Analyse regulatorischer Entwicklungen und ihre Auswirkungen auf Big Tech

properties.title: Global South Perspektive
properties.description: Data Colonialism, Content Moderation, Ausbeutung im Globalen Süden

properties.name: Digital Climate Impact
properties.definition: CO2-Fußabdruck digitaler Infrastruktur: Data Center, Netzwerke, Endgeräte. Ca. 3-4% globaler Emissionen

properties.name: Data Center Energy Consumption
properties.definition: Stromverbrauch von Rechenzentren: Kühlung, Server, Redundanz. Wasserverbrauch für Kühlung. Standortwahl nach Energiepreisen

properties.name: E-Waste & Hardware Lifecycle
properties.definition: Elektronikschrott durch kurze Hardware-Lebenszyklen. Planned obsolescence. Recycling in Entwicklungsländern (Ghana, Nigeria). Gesundheitsrisiken

properties.name: Critical Minerals & Resource Conflicts
properties.definition: Seltene Erden, Lithium, Kobalt für Chips und Batterien. Abbau im Globalen Süden (DR Kongo, Chile). Konflikte um Ressourcen. Geopolitische Abhängigkeiten

properties.name: Green Colonialism
properties.definition: Grüne Energiewende in Europa/Nordamerika finanziert durch Ausbeutung im Globalen Süden. Landgrabbing für Solarparks, Bergbau für Batterien

properties.name: Environmental Justice & Tech
properties.definition: Ungleiche Verteilung ökologischer Kosten der Digitalisierung. Betroffene Communities haben keine Stimme bei Entscheidungen

properties.title: Kapitel: Ökologische Kosten der Digitalisierung
properties.description: Data Center Energy, E-Waste, Critical Minerals (Kobalt, Seltene Erden), Green Colonialism, Environmental Justice. Verbindung zu Plattformkapitalismus: wer trägt die externalisierten Kosten?
